_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 1
questions.prompt: What does `std::move` do to a unique_ptr?
choices: Transfers its ownership to the destination
Copies the owned object automatically
Deletes both pointers
Makes the object global
questions.explanation: Move transfers the unique ownership; the destination owns the object and the source becomes empty.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 2
choices: When several parts truly share ownership
For every local integer
Whenever a raw reference is enough
To avoid including memory
questions.explanation: A shared pointer models multiple owners whose lifetimes overlap, not ordinary temporary access to an object.

questions.title: Trace Memory Ownership
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The unique pointer owns the Student, move transfers that ownership into active, and cleanup happens automatically when active leaves scope. The resulting output is 'Ada'.

questions.title: Memory Ownership good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Memory Ownership?
choices: Prefer values and standard containers first.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Prefer values and standard containers first. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: Raw new separates allocation from cleanup. A unique pointer keeps both responsibilities in one object lifetime.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Shared pointer is not the default'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

`std::shared_ptr` uses a reference count and is appropriate when ownership is genuinely shared. Using it everywhere can hide unclear lifetime design and can leak cycles, so begin with values, containers, or unique ownership.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: `std::shared_ptr` uses a reference count and is appropriate when ownership is genuinely shared. Using it everywhere can hide unclear lifetime design and can leak cycles, so begin with values, containers, or unique ownership.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Memory Ownership?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Use make_unique for one dynamic owner.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use make_unique for one dynamic owner. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Memory Ownership?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Move unique ownership deliberately.
questions.explanation: Move unique ownership deliberately. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Memory Ownership?
choices: Use shared ownership only when the model requires it.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use shared ownership only when the model requires it. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The unique pointer owns the Student, move transfers that ownership into active, and cleanup happens automatically when active leaves scope. The output is 'Ada'.
